stories.headline: Iran's IRGC claims retaliatory drone and missile strikes on US bases in Kuwait, Bahrain and Jordan
stories.summary: After a second night of US "self-defense" strikes, Iran's IRGC said it attacked US military targets across the Gulf overnight, including the Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain, Kuwait's Ali Al Salem air base and Jordan's Azraq base, claiming 21 targets hit. US officials said nearly all projectiles were intercepted with no casualties; Kuwait briefly closed then reopened its airspace.

perspectives.keyFraming: Both sides trade strikes for a second day as the April ceasefire frays, with damage claims largely unverified.
perspectives.summary: NPR and RFE/RL reported CENTCOM conducted additional self-defense strikes on Iranian air-defense, surveillance and communications sites, after which Iran's IRGC claimed strikes on 21 US targets in Bahrain, Kuwait and Jordan. Independent confirmation of damage was limited; Kuwait said air traffic 'returned to normal' after a brief closure.

sources.title: U.S. launches a second day of strikes on Iran and Iran fires back at Gulf States
US And Iran Exchange Strikes For Second Day As Hormuz Blockade Tightens

perspectives.keyFraming: Iran widens retaliation across the region, putting Gulf states that host US forces back in the firing line.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera reported Iran claimed its largest strike hit the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain around 2:30am local, with drones launched at Kuwait's Ali Al Salem base and a long-range missile fired at Azraq in Jordan. The IRGC claimed it destroyed an F-35 hangar in Jordan, while Kuwait confirmed active air-defense interceptions.

sources.title: Iran war day 104: Iran attacks US bases, closes strait after Trump strikes

stories.headline: Trump says "I love the inflation" after May CPI hits 4.2%, a three-year high driven by Iran-war energy costs
stories.summary: The May 2026 CPI, released June 10, showed prices up 4.2% year-over-year — the fastest since April 2023 — with energy driving most of the 0.5% monthly rise amid the Iran war. Asked about it in the Oval Office, Trump replied "I love the inflation," predicting prices will fall once the war ends. Democrats led by Schumer's "Trumpflation" floor speech seized on the remark; Speaker Johnson called it "out of context."

perspectives.keyFraming: May CPI rose to a three-year-high 4.2% annually, with war-driven energy costs the main driver, and Trump waved off the report.
perspectives.summary: BLS data released June 10 showed CPI up 0.5% in May and 4.2% over 12 months, the highest since April 2023, with core CPI at 2.9%. Energy accounted for most of the monthly increase, reflecting war-driven oil prices. Trump told reporters "No, I love it. The numbers were great," and predicted inflation would "come down like a rock" once the war ends.

sources.title: Trump says Iran will 'pay the price' and claims they have 'taken too long' to agree to a deal

perspectives.keyFraming: Democrats cast the remark as proof Trump is indifferent to families’ cost-of-living pain and a midterm gift.
perspectives.summary: Schumer used a Senate floor speech to say "Trumpflation is getting worse and worse with no end in sight." Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker posted that families "can't afford to feed their families" while "your struggle is a joke to him," and other Democrats amplified the clip, with strategists predicting attack ads.

sources.title: Trump says 'I love the inflation' as annual rate jumps to a 3-year high
Schumer criticizes Trump over rising inflation

perspectives.keyFraming: The White House and Speaker Johnson said the comment was out of context and that prices will fall once the Iran war ends.
perspectives.summary: Johnson told reporters the remark was "totally out of context," arguing Trump meant the current figure will look good against lower post-war numbers. Trump said the reading was "much lower than anticipated" given the war and would drop further once it ends.

sources.title: Johnson defends Trump inflation comment: 'Totally out of context'

stories.headline: Iran's IRGC declares Strait of Hormuz closed to all vessels after second night of US strikes; CENTCOM disputes it
stories.summary: Early on June 11, Iran's IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz closed to all vessels including oil tankers, warning any ship attempting passage would be fired on, and said it had struck two ships. US CENTCOM disputed the closure, saying the strait remains open and commercial traffic continues. Brent crude pushed above $96 a barrel after a second night of US strikes on Iranian coastal sites.

perspectives.keyFraming: Iran's total-closure declaration collides with the US military's public refutation, leaving the status of a chokepoint for a fifth of global oil contested.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera, NBC News and Seatrade Maritime reported the IRGC declared the strait closed to all vessels and claimed it hit two ships, while the US military publicly refuted the closure and said traffic continued. Roughly 20% of the world's seaborne oil normally moves through Hormuz, so even a disputed closure carried an immediate war premium into crude prices.

sources.title: Iran attacks Bahrain, Kuwait, Jordan and Hormuz ships after new US strikes
Iran claims it hit two ships, declares Strait of Hormuz closed
Live updates: U.S. launches 2nd day of strikes; Iran says Strait of Hormuz closed

perspectives.keyFraming: Iranian and Russian state media present the closure as a sovereign security measure forced on Tehran by repeated US attacks.
perspectives.summary: TASS relayed Iran's top military command declaring the strait shut to all traffic, framing it as a legitimate defensive response to US strikes on Iranian territory rather than an offensive blockade. State coverage emphasized Iranian resolve and warned foreign vessels away.

sources.title: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz for all vessels — top brass

perspectives.keyFraming: Gulf-region coverage foregrounds the maritime and energy-trade fallout of a contested closure on the region's economies.
perspectives.summary: Gulf News reported Iran's declaration that the strait was closed to all ships and oil tankers, stressing the consequences for the roughly fifth of global oil and much of the world's LNG that transits the waterway. Regional outlets highlighted shipping disruption and insurance costs over great-power messaging.

sources.title: Iran declares Hormuz Strait 'closed' to all ships, oil tankers

stories.headline: Oil extends gains as second day of US strikes on Iran and Hormuz closure claim tighten supply; Brent tops $96
stories.summary: Crude swung sharply on June 11: Brent topped $96 and WTI neared $92 early on Iran’s closure claim, then reversed lower (Brent toward ~$92, WTI toward ~$89) after the US said it had completed its latest strikes on Iran and ceasefire hopes revived. A seventh straight EIA inventory draw and the EIA’s ~$105 Brent June-July projection kept a supply bid under the market.

perspectives.keyFraming: The war premium ebbs as the US says its strikes are "completed" and diplomacy comes back into view, even with the strait still effectively shut.
perspectives.summary: After an early spike on Iran’s IRGC total-closure claim, Brent pulled back toward ~$92 and WTI toward ~$89 once the US said it had completed its latest strikes on Iran, reviving negotiation hopes; a seventh straight US inventory draw still tightened balances and the EIA’s STEO assumes Hormuz stays shut to most traffic near-term, projecting ~$105 Brent for June-July.

sources.title: Oil rises nearly 1% as US launches new strikes against Iran, supply tightens
Oil Prices Jump After U.S. Strikes Iranian Missile Sites

perspectives.keyFraming: US officials stress that exports and Gulf traffic are recovering, casting the strikes as self-defense rather than a deliberate energy shock.
perspectives.summary: OilPrice.com relayed US Energy Secretary Chris Wright's message that vessel traffic and Strait exports were rising despite the disruption, presenting the strikes as proportional responses to the downing of a US helicopter. The administration line sought to reassure markets that physical flows were not being fully severed even as Iran declared the strait closed.

sources.title: Strait of Hormuz Constraints Keep Oil Prices Elevated
